title: Pentagon's AI-First Military Takes Shape as Chip Wars Intensify
subhead: The US military locked in classified AI deals with seven major firms, while Huawei's surging chip sales and Nvidia's China retreat signaled a deeper fracturing of the global semiconductor order.

sections.heading: Pentagon Builds AI-First Military
sections.body:
The US Defense Department reached agreements with seven AI companies—OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, Amazon Web Services, Nvidia, SpaceX's xAI, and the startup Reflection—to deploy their AI systems on classified military networks. The Pentagon said these deals "accelerate the transformation toward establishing the United States military as an AI-first fighting force," integrating AI into "Impact Levels 6 and 7" environments—the highest classification tiers.

Anthropic was conspicuously excluded after refusing to loosen "red lines" around mass domestic surveillance and fully autonomous lethal weapons. The Pentagon designated Anthropic a supply-chain risk in March, barring its products from federal networks. The company sued and won a temporary injunction, but the blacklist stands. The Defense Department's chief technology officer called Anthropic's new cybersecurity model, Mythos, a "separate national security moment"—acknowledging its power even as the company remains frozen out.

The Pentagon has requested $54 billion for autonomous weapons development alone. These agreements formalize what was already happening in practice: the major AI labs are now defense contractors, their models embedded in warfighting infrastructure from drone targeting to intelligence synthesis.
sections.whyItMatters: Structural stakes: The US military has formally absorbed the leading AI labs into its classified infrastructure, while the one company that objected to certain military uses has been cut out and punished. The line between commercial AI and defense AI is gone. This consolidates US military AI advantage but raises questions about civilian oversight and the exportability of these systems to allies.

sections.heading: Nvidia's China Exit Creates Huawei Opening
sections.body:
Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang said the US should "export like crazy" and argued that past export controls "have already largely backfired." He said Nvidia's market share in China has dropped from "90 some-odd percent" to "zero" after Biden-era chip restrictions. "Conceding an entire market the size of China probably doesn't make a lot of strategic sense," Huang told the Special Competitive Studies Project.

Meanwhile, Huawei expects its AI chip revenue to jump at least 60% this year, with the Financial Times reporting strong demand from Chinese AI firms. Huawei is on track to become China's top AI chip supplier as Nvidia's H200 shipments stall amid export restrictions and customs delays. The Trump administration has approved some H200 exports to China, but Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said Beijing isn't allowing its firms to buy them.

South Korea's exports rose 48% in April, driven by AI chip sales—evidence that global demand for advanced semiconductors remains intense, but the US-China channel is being rerouted through other suppliers and domestic Chinese alternatives.
sections.whyItMatters: Power shift: US export controls succeeded in blocking Nvidia from China but failed to prevent Chinese AI development. Huawei is filling the gap with domestic chips. The result is a bifurcated market: American chips for the US and its allies, Chinese chips for China and its partners. This wasn't containment—it was managed retreat.

sections.heading: Iraq's Pipeline Corridors Become Data Arteries
sections.body:
Major US hyperscalers—including Amazon, Google, Meta, and Microsoft—are routing data out of the Gulf through fiber-optic cables strung alongside Iraqi oil pipelines. IQ Networks, an Iraqi telecom, built the "Silk Route Transit" to provide an overland alternative to submarine cables through the Red Sea and Strait of Hormuz—both vulnerable during the Iran war.

When Iranian drones struck Amazon's UAE and Bahrain data centers in March, banking apps, payment platforms, and enterprise services across the region went offline. Amazon told customers to migrate workloads out of the Middle East. The Iraqi route, which runs from southern Iraq through Kurdistan to Turkey and onward to Europe, now carries live traffic for "most if not all the hyperscalers."

The land route is faster, too: 70 milliseconds versus 150 milliseconds through submarine cables, a difference that matters for financial transactions and AI applications. Cloud providers are buying "dark fiber"—raw strands they control end-to-end, with no government or third party able to see what travels through.
sections.whyItMatters: Infrastructure sovereignty: The war forced a rethink of Gulf data architecture. Iraq's pipeline corridors—built for oil—are now critical digital infrastructure for US cloud giants. This gives Iraq leverage it didn't have before and creates a new chokepoint in the global data map. Turkey's role as the gateway to Europe strengthens its position as a transit state.

sections.heading: GPS III Completes, GPS IIIF Already Inbound
sections.body:
The US Space Force launched the final GPS III satellite on April 21, completing a decades-long modernization program. The new constellation offers position data three times more accurate and eight times more jam-resistant than previous generations. The service is already preparing GPS IIIF—the "Follow-On"—with the first launch slated for May 2027, promising "60 times more anti-jam capabilities than legacy space vehicles."

But ground systems remain a problem. The Space Force canceled the Next Generation Operational Control System (OCX) after a 15-year, $6.3 billion effort that never worked properly. The service will rely on the existing AEP ground system, which has been repeatedly upgraded to handle GPS III's capabilities, including M-Code—the encrypted military signal.

The launch also demonstrated the Space Force's new "Rapid Response Trailblazer" protocol, which cut mission preparation time from six months to four after the service had to switch from ULA's grounded Vulcan rocket to SpaceX's Falcon 9 on short notice.
sections.whyItMatters: Military resilience: The US is modernizing its position, navigation, and timing infrastructure faster than adversaries can degrade it. But the $6.3 billion OCX failure shows that software and ground systems remain the weak link. China and Russia have invested heavily in GPS jamming and spoofing; GPS IIIF's anti-jam specs are a direct response to that threat.

sections.heading: AI-Powered Exploits Force Defensive AI Arms Race
sections.body:
Anthropic released Claude Security, a defensive tool designed to counter the threat posed by its own Mythos AI model—a system capable of finding vulnerabilities in well-tested software at unprecedented speed. "Mythos in the hands of attackers threatens a storm beyond the power of security teams to weather," Anthropic said. Major security firms including CrowdStrike, Microsoft Security, Palo Alto Networks, and SentinelOne are integrating Mythos capabilities into their platforms.

The speed advantage has flipped. What once took days of back-and-forth between security teams and engineers can now be reduced to "a single sitting." The time-to-exploit is compressing toward minutes. Defenders need AI just to keep pace with attackers using the same tools.

OpenAI published its own "Cybersecurity in the Intelligence Age" action plan, calling for the US government to treat AI companies as "frontline partners" in cyber defense. The assumption is that frontier AI models will inevitably find their way to criminals and nation-states; the question is who gets there first with defensive capabilities.
sections.whyItMatters: Asymmetric warfare: AI models that can find and exploit vulnerabilities at scale have changed the cybersecurity calculus. The offense gained a new weapon; now defense is scrambling to match it. This is an arms race in software form, where the same capabilities that protect critical infrastructure can be used to attack it.

sections.heading: US Pragmatism on Rare Earths Faces Chinese Dominance
sections.body:
The Trump administration's need to secure rare earth supplies is pushing US diplomacy toward pragmatism—even with countries where political ties are strained. The US International Development Finance Corporation provided $50 million in equity funding for the Phalaborwa Rare Earths Project in South Africa, extracting minerals from mining waste.

But China's dominance remains overwhelming. The US Export-Import Bank's proposed stockpiling initiative would initially source critical minerals "from anywhere in the world, including China," according to an official involved. Analysts warn the US cannot sustain major military operations without Chinese rare earth supplies—vital for F-35s, missiles, and other advanced weapons.

Kazakhstan is emerging as a potential alternative supplier, positioning itself as essential to the US-China minerals rivalry. But building processing capacity takes years. China still refines the majority of key critical minerals, and a single policy shift in Beijing can stall American advanced manufacturing.
sections.whyItMatters: Dependency reality: Despite years of talk about decoupling from Chinese rare earths, the US military-industrial base still runs on Chinese processing. The minerals are the raw material of modern warfare, and Beijing knows it. Washington is scrambling to build alternatives, but the gap between rhetoric and capability remains stark.

bottomLine: The single most important tech-power shift of the day: the Pentagon formalized its absorption of US AI labs into classified military infrastructure while Huawei filled the void Nvidia left in China. The US military got the AI-first force it wanted, but at the cost of consolidating domestic AI under defense control and accelerating Chinese chip independence. The global technology order is now formally split: American AI for the US war machine, Chinese chips for Chinese AI, and everyone else choosing which stack to depend on.
sources: The Guardian, The Verge, Reuters, Export Compliance Daily, Financial Times, Rest of World, Defense One, South China Morning Post, The Diplomat, SecurityWeek, MIT Technology Review, CNBC, Yahoo Finance, The Hill
